narration: Almost everyone pays their credit card on the due date. That is the worst possible day to pay if you want a high credit score. Here's why.
visual: Dark background. Large credit card SVG slides up. A big red X animates over the DUE DATE label. 'WRONG DAY' springs in bold red below.

narration: Your credit score tracks something called utilization — how much of your limit you're using. Bureaus snapshot that exact number on one specific day.
visual: Light background. Credit card with LIMIT and BALANCE. Animated red bar fills to 75%. 'BUREAUS SNAPSHOT THIS' label pulses in blue.

narration: That day is your statement close date — not your due date. It's typically ten to fifteen days earlier, and most people have never heard of it.
visual: Dark background. Timeline arrow animates left to right. Statement close date dot appears in blue with 'BUREAUS LOOK HERE' arrow. Due date dot appears in red with X. Gap labeled '10–15 days later'.

narration: Being above thirty percent utilization can cut fifty points off your score. The sweet spot is actually under ten percent — that's where scores jump the most.
visual: Light background. Three zone bars reveal sequentially: 0–10% green OPTIMAL (+30 to +50 pts), 10–30% amber ACCEPTABLE, 30%+ red DANGER ZONE (–30 to –60 pts). '50-POINT SWING' springs in red.

narration: That fifty-point swing on a thirty-year mortgage adds twenty-one thousand dollars in extra interest. One timing mistake, six figures of damage.
visual: Dark background. House SVG floats in. Two boxes: green (Score 760+, 6.5% rate, $412K interest) vs red (Score 700, 7.2% rate, $433K interest). '$21,000 DIFFERENCE' explodes in gold.

narration: The fix takes two minutes. Find your statement close date in your card app, pay the balance down before that date, and watch your score update in thirty days.
visual: Light background. Phone SVG with animated rising credit score bar. Three-step checklist reveals: 1 Find statement close date, 2 Pay before that date, 3 Score updates in 30 days. 'FOLLOW FOR DAILY MONEY HACKS' CTA.
